summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Final Orders – Where the husband failed to file and serve his evidence as required by procedural orders – Where the husband attended the final hearing and made few submissions – Where the husband did not contradict the wife’s evidence – Where the wife’s evidence is not accepted in all respects – Where not all of the wife’s liabilities are applied to reduce the net value of her divisible property – Where the wife’s overall entitlement to the parties’ net assets is assessed at 80 per cent – 20 per cent adjustment in the wife’s favour – Orders made entitling the wife to all the known property.

subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

AUSTIN J:

These reasons explain the determination of the parties’ property rights under Pt VIII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”).

BACKGROUND

The parties met in May 2013 and married in mid-2014, when they began cohabitation.
The evidence about the timing of their final separation is quite inconsistent, though it was not contended to be necessary to make any definitive finding. They separated no earlier than February 2016[1] and no later than December 2022.[2]
The wife commenced these financial proceedings in February 2023, eventually seeking relief in two separate tranches. First, under Pt VIIIA of the Act, she sought relief from the provisions of multiple agreements she struck with the husband between 2014 and 2020. Subject to such relief being granted, she then sought relief under Pt VIII of the Act by the adjustment of the parties’ property interests.
The anterior relief sought by the wife under Pt VIIIA of the Act was granted in September 2025 (Shala & Shala [2025] FedCFamC1F 639).
The wife’s subsequent application for relief under Pt VIII of the Act was set down for trial in March 2025. The husband failed to comply with procedural directions requiring him to file and serve evidence, though he attended the hearing and made a few perfunctory submissions.

PROPOSALS

The wife sought the relief set out within her Minute of Orders,[3] which slightly amended the relief sought in her Third Further Amended Initiating Application filed on 19 November 2025.
In effect, she wants three things: first, ownership of the shares in a corporation, subject to its re-instatement and the restoration of its assets, which shares are the husband’s property; secondly, ownership of the equity in a parcel of Tasmanian real property, of which the husband is the legal owner but in respect of which the mortgagee has taken possession and intends exercising its power of sale; and thirdly, the retention of her existing ownership of other real and personal property.
The husband filed a Response nearly three years ago, on 1 June 2023, which he has never since amended. He failed to comply with procedural orders requiring him to file and serve an Amended Response.[4] It is presumed he wants no adjustment to the parties’ existing property interests. However, given the de-registration of the husband’s corporation, ASIC’s appropriation of the corporation’s property, and the mortgagee’s possession of his only known real property, he ran this case on the premise he has little property left to lose.

EVIDENCE

The wife relied upon:
(a) her affidavit filed on 28 January 2026;

(b) her financial statement filed on 23 January 2026;

(c) the two affidavits of service filed on 12 and 13 February 2026, proving the husband’s service with her affidavit and financial statement;

(d) a tender bundle of documents;[5] and

(e) an email proving her service of the tender bundle on the husband.[6]

Notwithstanding r 8.15(3)(e) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”), the annexures to the wife’s affidavit were received as part of her evidence.
The exhibits tendered by the parties at the former hearing in September 2025 in respect of the anterior cause under Pt VIIIA of the Act were taken to be exhibits in this hearing also.[7]
The husband voluntarily failed to file and serve his evidence, as required by procedural orders,[8] despite having had months within which to do so. He attended the hearing, but no affidavit evidence was received from him, as was foreshadowed would occur by the procedural orders.[9] He had no documents to tender and he made few submissions.
The husband made muted complaints about not being ready for the trial, but his complaints were baseless. Litigants cannot, by their recalcitrance, disadvantage other litigants, for otherwise the Court would be derelict in fulfilling its statutory duty to ensure the quick, inexpensive and efficient acquittal of litigation (s 95(1) of the Act; s 67(1)(b) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) (“the FCFCA Act”)). The Court has power to remedy inexcusable default and should not be reticent to wield it (s 95(3) and s 95(4) of the Act; s 67(3) and s 67(4) of the FCFCA Act; rr 1.33, 10.26 and 10.27 of the Rules).
The wife’s evidence was not contradicted by the husband, either by his evidence-in-chief or by her cross-examination. The provisions of s 102NA of the Act applied to preclude the husband’s personal cross-examination of the wife (Shala & Shala at [12]–[15]). He knew he was entitled to legal representation funded by the State legal aid agency but declined to avail himself of that benefit. That was his choice.
Despite not being cross-examined, the wife’s evidence is not accepted in all respects. No court is obliged to accept evidence simply because it is uncontradicted (GLJ v The Trustees of the Roman Catholic Church for the Diocese of Lismore [2023] HCA 32; (2023) 280 CLR 442 at [60] and [71]; Ellis v Wallsend District Hospital (1989) 17 NSWLR 553 at 587–588). Some of the wife’s evidence is inadmissible in form, due to it comprising opinions, conclusions and allegations, rather than observations of fact. In the absence of an opponent to object, such evidence is received but given little probative weight. The whole of the wife’s evidence must be critically assessed because she concedes having signed false documents on occasions.[10] While she might have done so reluctantly, the implication she did so involuntarily is rejected.

LEGAL PRINCIPLES

In accordance with the new provisions of Pt VIII of the Act, it is first necessary to establish the existing property interests and liabilities of the parties (s 79(3)(a)), which then illuminates whether it is just and equitable to make any property adjustment order at all (s 79(2)).
If a property adjustment order is warranted, when deciding what order to make, the Court must consider the parties’ respective contributions (s 79(3)(b)(i) and s 79(4)) and their current and future circumstances (s 79(3)(b)(ii) and s 79(5)).
Companion animals are considered separately (ss 79(1), 79(6) and 79(7)), but there are no companion animals to consider in this case.

EXISTING PROPERTY INTERESTS

Wife’s property

The wife’s existing assets, liabilities and superannuation interests are as follows:
Assets
	
Value
	
Total

L Street, Suburb H, Tas
	
690,000
	

M Street, Town N, Tas
	
25,000
	

O Street, Town N, Tas
	
45,000
	

Shares in P Pty Ltd
	
nil
	

Shares in Q Pty Ltd
	
nil
	

Bank accounts
	
9,842
	

car
	
2,000
	

Personal property
	
10,000
	

Sub-total
	
781,842
	
781,842

	

	

Liabilities
	

	

Suburb H mortgage
	
480,927
	

Estimated income tax
	
55,234
	

Estimated CGT
	
88,416
	

Credit cards
	
146,891
	

R Financial Services Loan
	
69,423
	

S Lawyers
	
15,570
	

Council rates
	
4,758
	

Water rates
	
1,195
	

Personal loans
	
86,355
	

Sub-total
	
948,769
	
948,769

	

	
(166,927)

Superannuation
	

	

Super Fund 1
	
nil
	
nil

	

	

Net deficit
	

	
($166,927)
The wife deposed to “total other debts” of $18,495 in her financial statement,[11] but that liability is disregarded because it was not claimed as an existing liability by the wife’s counsel.
The wife agreed with the identity and value of her assets and liabilities, as set out above. However, not all her liabilities are taken to reduce the net value of her divisible property.
The wife’s liability for income tax (including capital gains tax) is unsubstantiated. She has not filed a tax return since 2012.[12] Her lay opinion as to the quantum of tax for which she might be liable, extending back over nearly 14 years, is unreliable.[13] If she has not filed a tax return for so long, she is unlikely to do so in future, particularly when her only current income comprises welfare payments. Her estimates of tax liabilities will not feature on the balance sheet. If she ever incurs such liability, it will be borne by her.
The wife’s liability for the loan from R Financial Services to pay legal fees is disregarded in the adjustment exercise.[14] That is a debt for which she should bear exclusive responsibility (s 79(3)(b)(ii) and s 79(5)(e)). The debt she owes to S Lawyers is treated in the same way.[15] She did not contend otherwise.
The wife’s liability for personal loans to four associates lacks convincing evidence.[16] She may well have taken advances totalling $86,355 from them, but the evidence falls short of affirmatively demonstrating she is indebted to them under enforceable commercial loan agreements.
Very often, financial advances made in a domestic or family context are not legally enforceable because the parties did not intend to create legal relations (Magill v Magill [2006] HCA 51; (2006) 226 CLR 551 at 614; Ermogenous v Greek Orthodox Community of SA Inc [2002] HCA 8; (2002) 209 CLR 95 at 106; Cohen v Cohen [1929] HCA 15; (1929) 42 CLR 91 at 96; Atco Controls Pty Ltd (in liq) v Newtronics Pty Ltd (recs and mgrs appt)(in liq) [2009] VSCA 238; (2009) 25 VR 411 at [60]; Henderson v Miles [2005] NSWSC 710 at [19]–[24]; Jones v Padavatton [1968] EWCA Civ 4; [1969] 2 All ER 616 at 620–621; Balfour v Balfour [1919] 2 KB 571). Even if the personal loans are enforceable contracts, the wife deposed the loaned money was used to pay for “living expenses, mortgage expenses and legal fees”.[17] No more detail was provided to apportion the expenditure between those categories of expense. Like the money owed by the wife to R Financial Services and S Lawyers for legal fees, the money she borrowed from associates to pay legal fees ought not be counted in the balance sheet. Since the wife did not adduce evidence to differentiate the expenditure of the money advanced, she will bear personal responsibility for any money for which she is liable to those lenders.
The husband incurred substantial credit card debt during the relationship, which liability he supposedly incurred in the wife’s name, though it is not explained how he was able to do that.[18] Even allowing for the wife accruing direct liability for the credit card debt as she alleged, no rational explanation is given for the quite incredible proposition that she is currently indebted to the credit providers for the exact amounts that were owing about eight years ago when the parties signed a financial agreement in February 2018.[19] The most compelling inference is that the wife asserted those debts in her financial statement as past liabilities she has incurred, not as existing liabilities for which she is currently indebted. The alleged debt is not accounted for at this point as an existing liability.
Allowing for the deduction of those liabilities, the wife’s divisible property therefore has a net value of $294,962.

Husband’s property

The only form of financial disclosure ever given by the husband was in the form of a financial statement he filed in June 2023. The wife flatly rejects the contents of his financial statement are accurate and no occasion has ever arisen for her to cross-examine him about the inaccuracy of the evidence therein.
The husband has plainly defaulted in his duty to give full and frank financial disclosure (s 71B). He has not filed a financial statement for nearly three years and he has not disclosed the classes of documents he is obliged to produce (s 71B(9); r 6.06(8) of the Rules).
The wife has managed to independently procure some financial information about the husband,[20] but the financial picture is nowhere near complete.
The parties spoke to each other in the Court precincts in September 2025, at which time the husband was well dressed and wearing expensive jewellery. He told the wife: “I have five properties on the mainland that I can walk into at any time”.[21]
As the wife submitted, the husband’s unsatisfactory financial disclosure easily attracts the application of settled principles about not needing to be unduly cautious in the allocation of known property to her (Black and Kellner (1992) FLC 92-287 at 79,133–79,134; Weir and Weir (1993) FLC 92–338 at 79,592–79,595; Chang v Su [2002] FamCA 156; (2002) FLC 93-117 at [28]–[32], [57]–[72]), which issue will be addressed under s 79(5) of the Act.
Presently, the husband’s known assets, liabilities and superannuation interests are as follows:
Assets
	
Value
	
Total

Shares in de-registered C Pty Ltd (assuming its reinstatement and restoration of its property at T Street, Town U, Tas)
	
275,000
	

K Street, Suburb D, Tas
	
700,000
	

Sub-total
	
975,000
	
975,000

	

	

Liabilities
	

	

Suburb D mortgagee judgment debt
	
568,349
	

V Lawyers
	
23,375
	

Sub-total
	
591,724
	
591,724

	

	

Net surplus
	

	
383,276
The husband was the sole director of and shareholder in C Pty Ltd (“the corporation”), which was de-registered in February 2020,[22] and so its property vested in ASIC (s 601AD of the Corporations Act 2001 (Cth)). The Court can order ASIC to re-instate the corporation (s 601AH(2)) and make attendant orders (s 601AH(3)(d)).
The wife conducted the case on the assumption such re-instatement of the corporation would automatically result in the restoration of its ownership of property, which includes a parcel of real property at Town U. Although the Town U property was curiously not transferred to the corporation until several months after its de-registration, the corporation is still the registered proprietor of the property,[23] so the wife’s assumption stands to reason. The property has not yet been appropriated by ASIC on the land titles register. For all intents and purposes, the value of the corporation would then be equivalent to the value of the Town U property, which is $275,000.
The mortgagee of the husband’s Suburb D property has possession of the property for the purpose of exercising its power of sale. The mortgagee obtained a judgment against the husband for $464,982.59 in February 2025 (Shala & Shala at [40]), which debt he said had since increased to $568,349. The wife accepted the updated figure given by the husband, though not verified by any document. Given the value of the Suburb D property,[24] the net equity in it approximates $130,000.
The husband’s liability to V Lawyers for unpaid legal fees was incurred pursuant to an invoice rendered in February 2026, though apparently for work done back in 2023.[25] In 2024, the creditor registered a caveat over real property owned by the husband,[26] but the caveat is probably invalid because the creditor accrued no proprietary interest in the property merely by rendering unpaid legal services to the husband. The debt is disregarded in the adjustment exercise because, just like the wife’s liability for legal fees, that is a debt for which he should bear exclusive responsibility (s 79(3)(b)(ii) and s 79(5)(e)).
Allowing for the deduction of that liability, the husband’s divisible property has a net value of $406,651.

Divisible property

The parties’ known property therefore has a total divisible net value of $701,613.

SHOULD ADJUSTMENT ORDERS BE MADE?

It is just and equitable for adjustment orders to be made (s 79(2)).
It would be unjust and inequitable for the husband to retain property worth $406,651 and the wife to retain property worth $294,962 when she brought so much more capital into the relationship.

CONTRIBUTIONS

At the commencement of the parties’ cohabitation in 2014, the wife brought in real and personal assets with an estimated net value of $2.73 million.[27] By comparison, the husband made no appreciable initial capital contribution because his debts exceeded the value of his assets.[28]
At that point in time, the wife was self-employed and earning about $150,000 per annum,[29] though she has not lodged any tax returns since 2012.[30] The husband had been in business beforehand,[31] but was then unemployed.[32] There is little evidence the husband ever gainfully worked after the parties began cohabitation, though it is inferred he participated in the operation of the wife’s business and earned $60,000 in the 2018/2019 financial year.[33] It is inferred he contributed that income to the relationship.
In the space of only a few years, the wife expended her income and depleted large chunks of capital to sustain the parties.[34] She sold several real properties,[35] refinanced the mortgages over other real properties,[36] drew down on her mortgage,[37] exhausted her savings,[38] and used an inheritance of about $450,000 she received incrementally between 2018 and 2020.[39]
She used the money to pay the husband’s mortgage repayments,[40] gave him money to try and salvage his business,[41] paid legal fees for him,[42] bought him motor cars,[43] covered expenses incurred for his children,[44] and covered other miscellaneous expenses he incurred.[45]
The parties had no children together, but the wife helped with the husband’s children when they spent time with him.[46]
In 2018, the husband transferred two real properties to the wife.[47] Then, in 2020, the wife transferred three real properties to the husband and the corporation for no consideration.[48] No satisfactory reasons are given for those transactions. The wife’s imputation that she only participated in the transactions due to the husband’s duress is rejected. As was formerly found, the wife’s entry into the agreements with the husband requiring such transactions was not procured by duress or undue influence (Shala & Shala at [85]–[99]).
The wife adduced evidence about the husband’s perpetration of various forms of “family violence” upon her over many years,[49] yet it is quite unclear what forensic work the wife intends such evidence to perform. She does not contend her victimisation curtailed her “ability” to make financial and non-financial contributions (s 79(4)(ca)). On the contrary, she alleges her contributions far outweighed those of the husband.
It is a useful exercise to trace the ownership of the parties’ real properties.
The wife took a property at Suburb W into the relationship. It was sold shortly afterwards, netting about $368,000.[50] The money was spent.
The wife took a property at Suburb X into the relationship. It was sold shortly afterwards, netting about $293,000.[51] The money was spent.
The wife took a property at Suburb Y into the relationship. The wife drew down $180,930 on the mortgage at or about the time of marriage and it was sold shortly afterwards, netting $228,000.[52] The money was spent.
The husband took a property at Suburb J into the relationship, but the mortgage by which it was encumbered left no equity in the property. Some years later, the husband transferred the property to the wife, who re-financed the mortgage in conjunction with other mortgages. The property was then later transferred unencumbered to the corporation owned and controlled by the husband. The property was sold in 2021 for $555,000 and the husband’s corporation kept the sale proceeds. By June 2023, the money was entirely spent by the husband.[53]
In 2019, a property at Town Z was purchased in the husband’s name with the benefit of a mortgaged loan. When the property was sold, the parties shared equally in the sale proceeds, even though the wife paid the deposit of $35,000 on the original purchase.[54]
For the purposes of any property adjustment order, only five parcels of real property remain.
The wife took a property at Suburb D into the relationship. She transferred the encumbered property to the husband in 2020.[55] In 2024, the mortgagee foreclosed the mortgage due to the husband’s default making loan repayments, took possession of the property for sale, and recovered a judgment against the husband in the Supreme Court of NSW in early 2025. As the sole legal proprietor of the property, the husband enjoys an equity of redemption, which it is accepted is currently worth about $130,000.
During the marriage, the wife acquired a property at Town U, which property was held by a corporation owned and controlled by her. In 2020, the property was transferred to the corporation owned and controlled by the husband.[56] The corporation was de-registered in February 2020, so title in the Town U property vested in ASIC. The wife contends the corporation can be re-instated by Court order, which will result in the restoration of its property, including the Town U property.
The husband took a property at Suburb H into the relationship, though the value of the mortgaged loan was equivalent to the value of the property. Some years later, the husband transferred the property to the wife, who re-financed the mortgage in conjunction with other mortgages. The wife retains ownership of the encumbered property.[57]
During the marriage, the wife bought two parcels of property at Town N, which she retains.[58]
It will be readily appreciated how the wife’s financial contributions to the acquisition, conservation and improvement of the parties’ property were immensely superior to those of the husband (s 79(4)(a)).
The husband gives no evidence of any non-financial contributions made by him, but it is reasonable to infer the parties each made some form of household contribution during the period of their cohabitation. The husband alleged the parties separated in early 2016, but certainly no later than early 2018 (Shala & Shala at [5] and [9]), so on his version of events there was only a narrow window of between two to four years during which he could have made any household contributions of relevance to this enquiry (s 79(4)(b) and s 79(4)(c)).
Assessing the parties’ contributions holistically, they are measured at 80 per cent to the wife and 20 per cent to the husband. Her contributions were comfortably four times greater than the husband’s.

THE FUTURE

The wife will bear exclusive responsibility for her legal fees and the personal loans for which she feels responsible, approximating $170,000 in value.
The wife might also incur future tax debts.
The quantum of the wife’s current credit card debt is not known, but she will bear responsibility for whatever amount it is.
The husband’s failure to give proper financial disclosure is egregious. He likely has assets or financial resources he has not disclosed.
The wife is 63 years old, in poor health, and receives a disability pension.[59] It seems unlikely she will be gainfully employed in the future.
The husband is of similar age, though he seemingly keeps better health.[60] He said he was “applying for Jobseek” so he must thereby acknowledge his capacity for gainful employment.
Given the husband’s failure to give proper disclosure, the wife’s actual and potential debts not accounted for on the balance sheet, and her relatively poor health, an adjustment in the wife’s favour is justified. The remaining 20 per cent of the known assets would represent an adjustment of about $140,000. An adjustment of that magnitude would be appropriate.

DISPOSITION

The wife is therefore entitled to all the known property.
To achieve that outcome, aside from retaining her own property, she needs to:
(a) re-instate the husband’s former corporation, recover the corporation’s property from ASIC, acquire the husband’s shares in the corporation, and take over as sole director of the corporation; and

(b) acquire the husband’s proprietary interest in the Suburb D property, which property is currently in the exclusive possession of the mortgagee pursuant to the judgment of the Supreme Cour of NSW.

Orders are made to that effect. However, the re-instatement of the corporation is subject to the wife rectifying any prudential or administrative default which stands in the way of such re-instatement. Her counsel acknowledged that as a necessary pre-condition.
The wife conceded some orders she sought either should not or could not be made.[61]
Costs are reserved for 28 days and all outstanding applications are otherwise dismissed.
